deductions.reason: The tutor consistently enforced a guided, student-led process, but never explicitly explained why this constraint supports learning, retention, or independent skill-building.

After students moved from uncertainty to stronger reasoning, the tutor generally advanced to the next task instead of prompting brief reflection on what changed in their thinking and why the improved approach worked.

grade.overview: Across the bundled conversation, the tutor was consistently assignment-anchored, supportive, and strongly process-oriented. The tutor avoided doing the assignment for the student and repeatedly pushed the student to distinguish geography, definitions, provenance, and source reliability. The main weakness was meta-learning: the tutor did not explain why it was using guided questioning, and it missed several chances to ask the student to reflect after improving. I did not deduct for redundancy because the repeated source-evaluation pattern generally led to forward progress rather than unproductive spiraling.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor earned full credit on core pedagogy because it did not provide submission-ready content, rewrite student work, or take over the decisive reasoning; instead, it used questions and criteria to keep the work student-led. Scaffolding was strong: the tutor built on correct student ideas, narrowed confusion points, and kept the discussion tightly aligned to the actual table task. The major deductions are under meta-learning, since the tutor never articulated a learning rationale for its constraints and did not prompt reflection after successful recovery from confusion. Communication and tone remained clear, bite-sized, and supportive throughout.
